key_insight: When the inverse variable is represented fairly and consistently, adjoint methods excel for grid-based fields, while PINNs are more natural for neural parameterizations and can be cheaper on time-dependent problems.
engineering_relevance: For PDE-constrained estimation in mechanics, fluids, and materials, the right solver choice depends strongly on how the unknown is represented and how expensive trajectory storage and differentiation are.
method: Common abstract formulation with matched domain, PDE, observations, regularization, optimizer, parameterization, and precision
Head-to-head benchmarks on Burgers, Darcy, Allen--Cahn, and Navier--Stokes inverse problems
PINN warm-start for adjoint optimization to combine low-cost initialization with high-accuracy refinement
evidence_signal: The paper evaluates four PDE inverse benchmarks and reports that representation choice largely determines which method is preferred, with PINNs often reducing cost for time-dependent cases and warm-started adjoints recovering adjoint-level accuracy.
watch_item: Performance comparisons can change substantially if the parameterization, discretization, or optimizer differs between methods, so implementation parity is essential.
paper_title: Adjoint Method versus Physics-Informed Neural Networks in PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems
